source: CNBC Sharpe Angle — SPAC market structure, PIPE financing dynamics, retail investor trends
summary: Competition among IPO, direct listing, and SPAC pathways is net positive for public markets and will result in more companies going public
summary_extended: Baker argues that the proliferation of alternative going-public mechanisms — specifically SPACs and direct listings competing with traditional IPOs — is structurally beneficial for US public equity markets. His core evidence is that the number of publicly listed companies in the US has been roughly flat or declining over the past 20 years, which he views as a problem for investors. He contends that increased competition among listing pathways should lower barriers for companies to go public, thereby expanding the supply of investable public equities over time. The investment implication is broadly positive for equity markets as a whole rather than for any specific company or sector, making this a macro structural view rather than an actionable single-stock thesis. Baker does not cite specific beneficiary companies or quantitative targets, and his confidence is tempered by normative framing rather than predictive specificity.
detail: Baker argues that the proliferation of alternative going-public mechanisms (SPACs, direct listings) is structurally beneficial because the number of public companies in the US has declined over 20 years. More competition among listing methods should increase the supply of public companies, which is good for all equity investors.
confidence_evidence: Baker frames this as a structural view he holds but acknowledges uncertainty about outcomes; uses normative language ('awesome') rather than predictive specificity
quote_fragment: number of public companies in the United States is basically flat last 20 years
